Patch Set 1:

Ok, after looking in the code, it seems that though the server side uses client hints, the client will still be able to handle -ERANGE by falling back to the non-cached xattr code.

So the commit description is wrong, but the case seems to be correctly handled.

Indeed, it makes sense to avoid such cases by choosing a reasonable xattr size value (both for zfs and ldiskfs).

Patch Set 2:

Ok, I've fixed the commit message.

So the question is, is there a reasonable xattr chunk size so that xattr chunks for the vast majority of files will fit it?

Patch Set 2:

There should be some balance between pre-fetching xattr and RAM usage. For example, how much time will be used (by regular getxattr RPCs) if without xattr cache in average. If it is less than 3 ms and if most of the cases are regular sized EAs, then it is worth to apply your patch.

As for what is the best strategy about the xattr_cache RPC reply buffer, I have no idea. It depends on the applications usage statistics.

Patch Set 4:

"git rebase -i" generated broken code. I had to remove a conflicting line which sets RMF_ACL size.

As far as I can tell, there's no code either on the client or the server side which which fills out RMF_ACL or parses it for IT_GETXATTR requests. This field only formally exists in the GETXATTR protocol since the latter has to be compatible with RQF_LDLM_INTENT.

As far as I can tell, ACLs are protected with the lookup lock and transferred in OPEN RPCs, so there's no need to request them with GETXATTR at all.

> As far as I can tell, ACLs are protected with the lookup lock and transferred in OPEN RPCs

As well as GETATTR RPCs for open-less requests (e.g. lookup).

parents.subject: LU-8062 ptlrpc: increase sleep time in ptlrpc_request_bufs_pack()

commit.subject: LU-9417 mdc: excessive memory consumption by the xattr cache
commit.message:
LU-9417 mdc: excessive memory consumption by the xattr cache

The refill operation of the xattr cache does not know the
reply size in advance, so it makes a guess based on
the maxeasize value returned by the MDS.

In practice, it allocates 16 KiB for the common case and
4 MiB for the large xattr case. However, a typical reply
is just a few hundred bytes. Even worse, RHEL6 has a very
bad vmalloc()/vfree() design with global locks.

If we follow the conservative approach, we can prepare a
single memory page for the reply. It is large enough for
any reasonable xattr set and, at the same time, it does
not require multiple page memory reclaim, which can be
costly.

If, for a specific file, the reply is larger than a single
page, the client is prepared to handle that and will fall back
to non-cached xattr code. Indeed, if this happens often and
xattrs are often used to store large values, it makes sense to
disable the xattr cache at all since it wasn't designed for
such [mis]use.

Change-Id: I98d97ffea5c83bccbc9f254717af8d2c0ac4d77f
Signed-off-by: Andrew Perepechko <andrew.perepechko@seagate.com>
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/26887
Reviewed-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Ben Evans <bevans@cray.com>
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
